title: Probabilistic Guarantees to Explicit Constructions: Local Properties of Linear Codes
speaker: Nikhil Shagrithaya
abstract:
We present a general framework for derandomizing random linear codes with respect to a broad class of properties, known as local properties, which encompass several standard notions such as minimum distance, list-decoding, list-recovery, and perfect hashing. Our approach extends the classical Alon-Edmonds-Luby (AEL) construction through a modified formalism of local coordinate-wise linear (LCL) properties, introduced by Levi, Mosheiff, and Shagrithaya (FOCS 2025).
The main result shows that we can obtain explicit constructions of codes achieving LCL properties at the same level of optimality as random linear codes, with the cost of increased alphabet size. This gives the first explicit constructions of list-recoverable codes having output list sizes that match those of random linear codes, among other results.
In this talk we will give the necessary background required to formulate the main result, as well as a sketch of its proof.
Based on joint work with Fernando Granha Jeronimo.

proof_stages.label: Coding notions and list-recovery target
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up linear codes, rate, minimum distance, list decoding and list recovery, then places the goal of explicit constant-alphabet list-recoverable codes against known random-linear-code and Reed–Solomon benchmarks.

proof_stages.label: Bad-vector-set and LCL reformulation
proof_stages.summary: List decoding and list recovery are recast as avoiding forbidden finite sets of codewords, and this is generalized to local coordinate-wise linear properties specified by local profiles imposing row-wise linear constraints on star matrices.

proof_stages.label: Derandomization theorem and consequences
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: for every LCL property there is a polynomial-time explicit construction achieving rates arbitrarily close to the random-linear-code threshold, with the tradeoffs of larger alphabet size and linearity only over a subfield, yielding new list-recoverable and list-decodable code parameters.

proof_stages.label: Local-to-global construction framework
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy is organized around a small brute-force inner code carrying the desired local guarantee, a high-rate large-alphabet outer code, concatenation, and an expander-sampler permutation that transfers local properties to a long explicit code.

proof_stages.label: AEL distance-amplification template
proof_stages.summary: For the Alon–Edmonds–Luby minimum-distance construction, a contradiction proof pulls excessive agreement on the final code back through the sampler to many inner blocks, forces equality there by the inner code distance, and then contradicts the outer code distance.

proof_stages.label: Failure of the naive list-decoding transfer
proof_stages.summary: Applying the same template to list decoding shows the obstruction: ordinary inner list-decodability only forces two of four pulled-back codewords to coincide, which is too weak to contradict the high-rate outer code.

proof_stages.label: Strong inner-code guarantee and final contradiction
proof_stages.summary: The obstruction is overcome by proving, probabilistically and then brute-forcing at constant size, inner codes with a stronger agreement guarantee forcing all relevant codewords equal at the same threshold rate, so the expander transfer again contradicts the outer code and proves the derandomization theorem.

proof_stages.label: Scope and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion emphasizes the match between probabilistic and explicit methods and asks whether the approach can be extended beyond LCL properties, used for other pseudorandom objects, or improved in alphabet size.
